examples.title: CRC Card Structure for Class Design
examples.description: Pseudocode representation of a CRC card showing how to organize class information during analysis
examples.code:
CLASS: ClassName
========================
RESPONSIBILITIES:
 - Define what this class knows
 - Define what this class does
 - Define what it manages

COLLABORATORS:
 - ClassA (uses for...)
 - ClassB (provides...)
 - ClassC (depends on for...)

NOTES:
 - Additional design considerations
 - Constraints or rules
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the format of a CRC card. During requirements analysis, teams use these cards to brainstorm and organize classes. Each card represents one class with its responsibilities (what it does) and collaborators (other classes it interacts with).

examples.title: Class Definition with Attributes and Operations
examples.description: Example showing how to represent a class with attributes and operations in pseudocode
examples.code:
CLASS BankAccount
-- Attributes --
 - accountId: String
 - balance: Real
 - accountHolder: Customer
 - transactions: List<Transaction>

-- Operations --
 + deposit(amount: Real): Boolean
 + withdraw(amount: Real): Boolean
 + getBalance(): Real
 - validateTransaction(amount: Real): Boolean

-- Relationships --
 ASSOCIATION: Customer (1..*) ─── (0..1) BankAccount
 COMPOSITION: BankAccount ─── (1..*) Transaction

NOTES:
 - balance is private to ensure encapsulation
 - Transaction objects are destroyed when Account is closed
examples.explanation: This example shows a BankAccount class with various visibility levels for attributes and operations. The relationships section indicates: each BankAccount belongs to one Customer (1), and each BankAccount contains multiple Transactions (composition - transactions cannot exist without account).

examples.title: N-Queens Class Model Pseudocode
examples.description: Class design for solving the N-Queens problem using object-oriented principles
examples.code:
CLASS Position
-- Attributes --
 - row: Integer
 - column: Integer

-- Operations --
 + Position(r: Integer, c: Integer)
 + getRow(): Integer
 + getColumn(): Integer

CLASS ChessBoard
-- Attributes --
 - size: Integer
 - queens: List<Queen>

-- Operations --
 + initialize(size: Integer)
 + placeQueen(position: Position): Boolean
 + removeQueen(position: Position): Boolean
 + isSafe(position: Position): Boolean

CLASS Queen
-- Attributes --
 - position: Position

-- Operations --
 + Queen(position: Position)
 + canAttack(other: Queen): Boolean
 + getPosition(): Position

-- Collaborations --
 - ChessBoard (placed/removed by board)
 - Queen (checks attacks with other queens)
examples.explanation: This shows the class design for the N-Queens problem. Position stores coordinates, ChessBoard manages the overall solution process, and Queen represents each queen piece. The key collaboration is that each Queen must check if it can attack other Queens - this is a self-association where Queen collaborates with other instances of Queen.
